chunk_summary: Mechanism-based stool phenotype classification guides nutrition actions for chronic diarrhea in India, focusing on physiology-driven interventions to prevent harmful dietary cycles and support GI health during triage and assessment.
learning_objectives: Understand stool phenotype classification (watery, fatty, inflammatory) and its role in chronic diarrhea management.
Identify mechanisms of absorption failure (luminal, mucosal, post-mucosal) and their nutritional implications.
Apply physiology-driven nutrition strategies to avoid harmful empiric dietary restrictions.
Recognize India-specific considerations for inflammatory diarrhea, including intestinal TB and IBD.
key_facts: Chronic diarrhea is classified by stool phenotype: watery (osmotic, secretory, functional), fatty, and inflammatory.
Watery diarrhea: Osmotic patterns worsen with high carbohydrate loads; secretory persists during fasting; functional linked to excess juices/sorbitol and low dietary fat.
Fatty stools indicate fat maldigestion/malabsorption (e.g., pancreatic insufficiency, bile acid issues); avoid low-fat diets and support fat-soluble vitamins.
Inflammatory diarrhea (blood/mucus, fever, weight loss) requires urgent evaluation for IBD, infection, or intestinal TB in India.
Nutrition rules: Continue breastfeeding, avoid milk dilution, use mechanism-driven trials (e.g., low-lactose for secondary lactase deficiency) with explicit stop rules.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Persistent diarrhea requires mechanism-based classification to guide nutrition actions and prevent harmful empiric dietary cycles. (Source: IAP Standard Treatment Guidelines, Chapter 030)
